justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Distributed tracing gap explicitly identified as 'the single biggest debuggability gap' and cross-service diagnosis blocker, (2) 30-second log delay called out as creating 'a stale picture during a live incident', (3) 18-month stale runbook flagged as 'worse than no runbook', (4) No recovery tooling for stuck orders identified — 'every failed transition requires manual intervention', (5) Multiple specific recommendations including trace context propagation, runbook update, outbox/event log, staleness detection, and recovery tooling, (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough incident response assessment — goes well beyond monitoring coverage to assess debugging capability, recovery paths, and on-call experience
The debugging_capability narrative vividly describes the on-call engineer's experience ('20-40 minutes just building situational awareness at 3am'), demonstrating genuine incident response thinking
Correctly identifies alert routing as adequate while noting the lack of severity tiering
Identifies additional gaps beyond the rubric: missing staleness detection gauge, no circuit breakers, no timeout configuration, alert severity tiering
Recovery tooling gap is thoroughly articulated across multiple sections — outbox pattern, event log, dead-letter queue, reconciliation mechanism
Escalations are well-structured with clear reasoning for each

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Distributed tracing gap clearly identified as cross-service diagnosis blocker in observability_review.tracing and escalations. (2) 30-second log ingestion delay called out in debugging_capability as hindrance during first minutes of investigation. (3) Stale 18-month runbook flagged in on_call_documentation with strong language about it being 'worse than no runbook.' (4) No recovery tooling for stuck orders identified in resilience_assessment and escalations — manual DB intervention required. (5) Multiple specific recommendations: distributed tracing infrastructure, recovery CLI/admin endpoint, runbook update, stuck-order gauge metric, absence-of-signal alerting, outbox/DLQ. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough incident response assessment — goes well beyond monitoring coverage to evaluate what the on-call engineer actually experiences during an incident
Identifies subtle failure modes like silent stalls (no failures = no alerts) and absence-of-signal gaps that many assessments miss
Strong framing of the 3am on-call scenario throughout, connecting technical gaps to human impact on MTTR
Identifies the static alert threshold limitation (gradual degradation below 5/min never pages) as a bonus finding beyond the rubric requirements
Correctly notes PagerDuty alert routing is adequate while identifying the deeper incident response gaps
Excellent escalation quality — each escalation explains the incident scenario where the gap becomes critical, not just the technical absence

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Distributed tracing gap clearly identified as cross-service diagnosis blocker throughout the output. (2) 30-second log delay noted in assumptions as slowing real-time debugging. (3) Stale 18-month runbook explicitly flagged in operational_readiness and escalations. (4) No recovery tooling for stuck orders identified extensively across resilience_assessment and escalations. (5) Multiple specific recommendations: tracing infrastructure, dead-letter queue/replay, transactional outbox, runbook update, dwell-time monitoring. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough incident response assessment — goes well beyond monitoring coverage to evaluate diagnosis, recovery, and on-call experience
Excellent framing of the 3am on-call scenario, clearly distinguishing what the engineer CAN answer vs CANNOT answer
Correctly identifies PagerDuty alerting as adequate while flagging deeper gaps
Stale runbook analysis is nuanced — notes stale runbooks can be worse than no runbook due to misleading information
Recovery tooling gap is comprehensively covered with concrete recommendations (outbox pattern, dead-letter queue, batch replay)
The 30-second log delay is only mentioned in passing within assumptions rather than called out as a primary incident response concern — it deserved more prominence
